differenciálás

title: matematika mérnököknek II
text:
differenciálegyenletek

elsőrendű lineáris homogén

title: integrálás
sillabusz
text:
Differenciálegyenletek bevezetése példák segítségével (szabadesés-, harmonikus rezgőmozgás-, rádióaktív bomlás egyenlete).
Differenciálegyenletek osztályozása, n-edrendű, implicit/explicit közönséges differenciálegyenletek (KDE), elsőrendű közönséges differenciálegyenletek megoldása.
Kezdeti érték feltétel, illetve -probléma. Vonalelem, iránymező definíciója.
Elemi úton megoldható elsőrendű közönséges differenciálegyenletek.
Homogén/inhomogén lineáris differenciálegyenletek és megoldásuk.
Egyenletrendszerek, magasabb rendű egyenletek, átviteli elv.
Állandó együtthatós, másodrendű, homogén, lineáris differenciálegyenlet és azok karakterisztikus egyenlete, megoldása.
Differenciálegyenletek numerikus megoldása (fokozatos közelítés, Euler-, Runge-Kutta módszerek).
Magasabb rendű egyenletek numerikus megoldása.
Függvények közelítése polinomokkal: hatványsor, Taylor-sor és konvergencia sugár.
Fourier sorok, együtthatók kiszámolása általános, illetve páros és páratlan függvények esetén.
Fourier transzformált és inverz Fourier transzformált és azok tulajdonságai.
Diszkrét/Inverz diszkrét Fourier transzformáció.
Laplace transzformáció definíciója, tulajdonságai, kiszámítása és alkalmazása differenciálegyenletek megoldásához.
A fent megnevezett témakörökhöz kapcsolódó MATLAB ismeretek elsajátítása (iránymezők kirajzoltatása, első- és magasabb rendű differenciálegyenletek megoldása, hatványsorok, Fourier-sorok és Fourier-transzformáltak segítségével kapott közelítő függvények kirajzoltatása, Laplace transzformáltak meghatározása).

title: norm/concepts_draft
norm/concepts_draft
norm/concepts_draft
text:
review

in this section x,y \in \mathbb{R}^n and \alpha \in \mathbb{R}
how large is a vector?
it is denoted by \Vert x \Vert
requirements (axioms):

\Vert x\Vert\ge 0 and \Vert x\Vert=0\ \text{ iff }\ x=0
\Vert x+y \Vert \le \Vert x\Vert + \Vert y \Vert

it is the triangle inequality

\Vert \alpha \cdot x \Vert = \vert \alpha\vert \cdot \Vert x \Vert

the following vector norms are important

\Vert x \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{\vert x_1\vert^2 + \ldots + \vert x_n\vert^2}\ \  (euclidean or 2-norm)
\Vert x \Vert_{1} = \vert x_1\vert + \ldots + \vert x_n\vert\ \  (manhattan or 1-norm)
\Vert x \Vert_{\infty} = \max_{i=k\ldots n}\vert x_k\vert\ \  (\infty-norm)

the distance between vectors: \Vert x-y\Vert

small: close to each other
large: far from each other

read this for more details

title: norm/concepts_draft
norm/concepts_draft
text:
matlab

norm(x,1), norm(x,2), norm(x,inf)

title: norm/concepts_draft
text:
properties

the following properties are also valid

\begin{gather*}
\Vert E \Vert = 1\\
\Vert A\cdot x\Vert \le \Vert A\Vert \cdot \Vert x \Vert\\
\Vert A\cdot B \Vert \le \Vert A\Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert\\
\end{gather*}

title: norm/concepts_draft
norm/concepts_draft
text:
computational rules

a_{ij} is j-th element of the i-th row for a matrix A


1-norm

\Vert A\Vert _1=\max\limits_{1\leq j\leq n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n\vert
a_{ij}\vert


for each column compute the sum of absolute values and take the maximal one



2 norm

\Vert A\Vert _2=\sqrt{\lambda _{\max} (A^{T}A)}


take the square root of the maximal absolute value eigenvalue of the matrix (A^{T}A)



\infty norm

\Vert A\Vert _\infty=\max_{1\leq i\leq n}\sum_{j=1}^n\vert a_{ij}\vert


for each row compute the sum of absolute values and take the maximal one

read this for details

title: norm/concepts_draft
text:
numerical-example

A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}
-3&0&4\\
1&-1&2\\
-2&1&-2
\end{array}\right]


\Vert A\Vert_{1}= 8
\Vert A\Vert_{\infty}= 7

title: norm/concepts_draft
text:
example (linsys)

compare the solutions of the linear systems below 
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
1&1\\
1&1.0001
\end{array}
\right]
\left[\begin{array}{r}
x_1\\
x_2
\end{array}\right] =
\left[\begin{array}{r}
2\\
2
\end{array}\right]


x=[2,0]^{T} 
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
1&1\\
1&1.0001
\end{array}
\right]
\left[\begin{array}{r}
y_1\\
y_2
\end{array}\right] =
\left[\begin{array}{r}
2\\
2.0001
\end{array}\right]

y=[1,1]^{T}

large difference, despite the matrix is the same and the right hand sides are very close to each other (in vector norm!)
how can we explain this behavior?

norm/concepts_draft

properties

its value does depend on the norm used
\text{cond}(A) \geq 1
for c\neq 0, \text{cond}(cA)=\text{cond}(A)

read this for more details

title: norm/concepts_draft
text:
ill-conditioned

if \text{cond}(A)*b_{\text{relative error}}\ge 1 the system is called ill-conditioned
it means that the absolute error in the solution can be as large as the true solution itself - it is bad.

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
here, every mentioned matrix is invertible n\times n, for a fixed n. (it is not neccessary to assume this everywhere, however)
LT(UT) is a short lower(upper) triangle
L(U)’s denote always LT(UT) matrices (with appropriate indexing,labeling)





invertible LT(UT) matrices have nonzeros in their main diagonal
the product of two LT(UT) matrices is LT(UT)
the i-th diagonal element of

L^{(1)}L^{(2)} is l^{(1)}_{ii}l^{(2)}_{ii}
U^{(1)}U^{(2)} is u^{(1)}_{ii}u^{(2)}_{ii}

the inverse of a LT(UT) matrix is a LT(UT) matrix





if A is invertible and A=LU with unit-diagonal L then this factorization is unique.

suppose that we have A=L_{1}U_{1}=L_{2}U_{2}.
L_{2}^{-1}L_{1}=U_{2}U_{1}^{-1}=D.
D is a diagonal matrix, for it is LT and UT by lemma 2. and 4.
d_{ii}=1 by lemma 3.

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
here, every mentioned matrix is invertible n\times n, for a fixed n. (it is not neccessary to assume this everywhere, however)
LT(UT) is a short lower(upper) triangle
L(U)’s denote always LT(UT) matrices (with appropriate indexing,labeling)

title: linsys/lu_related
linsys/lu_related
text:
if A is invertible and A=LU with unit-diagonal L then this factorization is unique.

suppose that we have A=L_{1}U_{1}=L_{2}U_{2}.
L_{2}^{-1}L_{1}=U_{2}U_{1}^{-1}=D.
D is a diagonal matrix, for it is LT and UT by lemma 2. and 4.
d_{ii}=1 by lemma 3.

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
definition

strictly diagonally dominant
a matrix A is called sdd if for each |a_{ii}| > \sum_{j\neq i}|a_{ij}| for each i

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
proposition

if A is sdd the A=LU
the key is to understand how this property is preserved during gaussian elimination.
it is enough to consider two vectors


\begin{gather*}
x\ \ \text{with} \ \ \ |x_1|>\sum_{i>1} |x_{i}| \\
y\ \ \text{with} \ \ \ |y_j|>\sum_{i\neq j} |y_{i}| \ \ j>i \\
\end{gather*}


we are going to show that the elimination step preserves the strictly dominant property


\begin{gather*}
\vert y_j - y_1 \frac{x_j}{x_1} \vert \ge
\vert y_j\vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert} = (1)\\
%
\sum_{i\neq j} \vert y_i - y_1 \frac{x_i}{x_1}\vert \le
\sum_{i\neq j,i\neq 1} \vert y_i\vert  + \sum_{i\neq j,i\neq 1}\vert y_1\vert \frac{\vert x_i\vert}{\vert x_1\vert} =\\
%
(2) + (3)+\vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert} - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=(4)\\
%
(3)+\vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=
\vert y_1\vert \frac{\sum_{i\neq 1} \vert x_i\vert}{\vert x_1\vert}<\vert y_1\vert\\
%
(4)<(2)+\vert y_1 \vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}=\\
%
\sum_{i\neq j} \vert y_i\vert - \vert y_1 \vert \frac{\vert x_j\vert }{\vert x_1\vert}<(1)
\end{gather*}

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
definition

in general minor is the determinant of a submatrix, where the same rows and columns are removed from the original matrix
the k-th leading principal minor of a n\times n matrix A is det(B) where B is created by removing the last n-k rows and columns from A

title: linsys/lu_related
text:
proposition

if each of leading principal minor is nonzero then A=LU
we cannot encounter zero in the main diagonal during the elimination because of the condition

title: linsys/concepts_draft
text:
setting up an appropriate SLE is the hardest part of the problem solving…but here we only deal with the “how to solve it” part.
our aim is to solve systems of linear equations (SLE’s)
we have m equations n variables, a cofficient matrix A and a right hand side b 
\begin{gather*}
a_{11}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{1n}x_{n} = b_{1}\\
a_{21}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{2n}x_{n} = b_{2}\\
\vdots{}\\
a_{m1}x_{1}+\ldots+a_{mn}x_{n} = b_{m}\\
\text{or}\\
Ax=b\\
\text{or}\\
x_{1}a_{.1}+x_{2}a_{.2}+\ldots+x_{n}a_{.n}=b\\
\end{gather*}

here by a_{.j} we mean the j-th column of A
the third form is the most expressive one

it tells us that we are working in vector spaces
it tells us that we are searching for a linear combination of the column vectors that is b and the solution for the system (if any) is coefficiant set of the combination.
from linear algebra we know that we have three cases:

we cannot express b by means of the given system (0 solutions)
we can express b uniquely (1-solution)
we can express b in several different ways (\infty-solutions)

title: linsys/concepts_draft
text:
matlab

the function rref can and should be used. it also performs the backward elimination steps and the rows are normalized.

title: linsys/concepts_draft
text:
example

for the computation below this program is used.







Noneclick to open





>> A=[4,1,-1;-1,8,0;1,1,4];
>> [L,U]=mylu(A)

phase 0:

   1   0   0   4   1  -1
   0   1   0  -1   8   0
   0   0   1   1   1   4

press a key to continue


phase 1:

  sub-phase 2:

    subtract -1/4 times the 1. row from the 2. row

     1     0     0     4     1    -1
  -1/4     1     0     0  33/4  -1/4
     0     0     1     1     1     4

press a key to continue


  sub-phase 3:

    subtract 1/4 times the 1. row from the 3. row

     1     0     0     4     1    -1
  -1/4     1     0     0  33/4  -1/4
   1/4     0     1     0   3/4  17/4

press a key to continue


phase 2:

  sub-phase 3:

    subtract 1/11 times the 2. row from the 3. row

      1      0      0      4      1     -1
   -1/4      1      0      0   33/4   -1/4
    1/4   1/11      1      0      0  47/11

press a key to continue


L =

       1              0              0       
      -1/4            1              0       
       1/4            1/11           1       


U =

       4              1             -1       
       0             33/4           -1/4     
       0              0             47/11

title: linsys/concepts_draft
text:
matlab

the function call [L,U,P]=lu(A) will create a PLU factorization for the parameter matrix. Almost always row-swaps will be performed.
the backslash (\) operator is a built-in for solving linear systems.







Noneclick to open





>> A=[4,1,-1;-1,8,0;1,1,4];b=[1;2;4];
>> x=A\b;
>> x

x =

    0.3830
    0.2979
    0.8298

>> A*x

ans =

    1.0000
    2.0000
    4.0000




it is important that the \ can return a non-solution. It tries to find the best possible answer and if there is no solution then it tries to minimize \Vert Ax - b\Vert_{2} (least square optimal solution):







Noneclick to open





>> A=[1 0; 2 3; 0 1]; b=[6;3;0];
>> x=A\b

x =

    4.7143
   -1.9286

>> A*x

ans =

    4.7143
    3.6429
   -1.9286


>> % we will learn we this form used:
>> norm(A'*A*x - A'*b)

ans =

   5.0243e-15

rank

that is: the dimension of the subspace generated by the column-vectors of A
count the number of non-zero elements in the main diagonal of U
for example the rank of the coefficient matrix of Listing 3 is : 3 (why?)

title: leastsquares/GN
text:
\Vert f-Ax \Vert_{2}^{2} \ \ \text{is minimal} \ \ \Longleftrightarrow \ \ A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f




a mechanic calculation
take the partial derivatives of J by x_k’s
solve the system \frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}J=0
the “solution” is A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f
A^{T}A is positive semi-definite matrix: the solution is a local minimizer.





it provides more insight to the background of the least squares-method
if Ax=f is solvable then the statement is trivial
lemma: if \langle y,Ax\rangle=0\ \ (\forall x) \ \ \Longrightarrow\ \ A^{T}y=0
let’s assume that f does not lie in the columns space of A
it follows from the Gram-Schmidt method that there is an \hat{x} for which f-A\hat{x} is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax
A\hat{x} is called orthogonal projection (…)
using the lemma one can prove that A^{T}A\hat{x}=A^{T}f
using the Pythagorean theorem

it follows that A\hat{x} is the unique minimizer of \Vert f-Ax\Vert_{2}.
if f-Ay is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax for some Ay then Ay=A\hat{x}.

we already know that \hat{x} minimizes J and it is a solution for A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f.
using the lemma again one can prove that if A^{T}Ay=A^{T}f for a y then Ay=A\hat{x}, that is it also y also a minimizer for J.

title: leastsquares/GN
text:
a mechanic calculation
take the partial derivatives of J by x_k’s
solve the system \frac{\partial}{\partial x_k}J=0
the “solution” is A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f
A^{T}A is positive semi-definite matrix: the solution is a local minimizer.

title: leastsquares/GN
text:
it provides more insight to the background of the least squares-method
if Ax=f is solvable then the statement is trivial
lemma: if \langle y,Ax\rangle=0\ \ (\forall x) \ \ \Longrightarrow\ \ A^{T}y=0
let’s assume that f does not lie in the columns space of A
it follows from the Gram-Schmidt method that there is an \hat{x} for which f-A\hat{x} is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax
A\hat{x} is called orthogonal projection (…)
using the lemma one can prove that A^{T}A\hat{x}=A^{T}f
using the Pythagorean theorem

it follows that A\hat{x} is the unique minimizer of \Vert f-Ax\Vert_{2}.
if f-Ay is orthogonal to an arbitrary Ax for some Ay then Ay=A\hat{x}.

we already know that \hat{x} minimizes J and it is a solution for A^{T}Ax=A^{T}f.
using the lemma again one can prove that if A^{T}Ay=A^{T}f for a y then Ay=A\hat{x}, that is it also y also a minimizer for J.

title: binary expansion of integers
binary expansion of fractions
text:
x \in [0,1)
the first expansion exists and unique, assuming that the tail of x_{n} is not a constant 1 sequence.
2x - x_{1} will be the “new” x

\begin{align}
x &= 2^{-1}x_{1}+2^{-2}x_{2}+\ldots+2^{-n}x_{n}+\ldots \\
2x &= x_{1}+ 2^{-1}x_{2}+\ldots+2^{n+1}x_{n}+\ldots \\
2x - x_{1} &= 2^{-1}x_{2}+\ldots+2^{n+1}x_{n}+\ldots
\end{align}

title: syllabus
text:
Topics

Floating point systems (properties, rounding, errors)
Systems of linear equation with error in the right-hand side, matrix norms, condition number.
Numerical solution of linear systems (LU,PLU,Cholesky)
Least square approximation.
Polynomial interpolation (Lagrange, Hermite, spline)
Numerical integration.
Numerical methods for solving of nonlinear equations and systems.
Optimization



Requirements for the practice grade

At most 3 absences from the labors.
There will a grading test at the end of the study period, and it will consist both numerical/multiple choice/matlab questions.
The grade will be determined by the following evaluation system:

0% - 50% \longrightarrow 1
50% - 70% \longrightarrow 2
70% - 85% \longrightarrow 3
85% - 95% \longrightarrow 4
95% -100% \longrightarrow 5

The exact date of the test will be announced on the e-learning site.
The retake will be held in the exam period.
The sample test collections on the e-learning site and the shared exercises on this site are serving as the basis for the grading test.
In the study period at the time of lectures there will be some extra tests. The points obtained by these tests can be used to increase your grading test’s result.

title: About
text: Some education related pages.

title: syllabus
text:
Topics

Floating point systems (properties, rounding, errors)
Systems of linear equation with error in the right-hand side, matrix norms, condition number.
Numerical solution of linear systems (LU,PLU,Cholesky)
Least square approximation.
Polynomial interpolation (Lagrange, Hermite, spline)
Numerical integration.
Eigenvalue problems, sparse systems.
Numerical solution of nonlinear equations and systems, minimization.
Linear programming (graphical and 1,2-phase simplex method) Duality in linear programming, sensitivity analysis. Algorithms for solving transportation and assignment problems.



Requirements for the practice grade

At most 3 absences from labors. At most 3 absences from practices. Note that the practice is mandatory.
There will a grading test at the end of the study period, and it will consist both numerical/multiple choice/matlab questions.
The grade will be determined by the following evaluation system:

0% - 50% \longrightarrow 1
50% - 70% \longrightarrow 2
70% - 85% \longrightarrow 3
85% - 95% \longrightarrow 4
95% -100% \longrightarrow 5

The exact date of the test will be announced on the e-learning site.
The retake will be held in the exam period.
The sample test collections on the e-learning site and the shared exercises on this site are serving as the basis for the grading test.
In the study period at the time of practices there will be some extra tests. The points obtained by these tests can be used to increase your grading test’s result.

title: exercises_draft
text: representing numbers

title: exercises_draft
text:
important numbers

let {\cal F}=\{a=2, k_{-}=-3, k_{+}=3, t=4\}

compute the (normalized) representations for 0.2,\ 1.45,\ \pi in \cal F using regular rounding
what is \varepsilon_{0},\ \varepsilon_{1},\ M_{\infty},\ 1_{+},\ 1_{-} in \cal F
how many positive numbers \cal F has?
what is the smallest positive integer that is not in \cal F?

these are double system related exercises

write a function that returns \varepsilon_{0}!
write a function that returns \varepsilon_{1}!
write a function that returns x_{+}, given x!
verify, that (x+y)+z \neq x+(y+z) for x=0.4,\ y=-0.5, z=0.1

does it occur frequently?

let x=\frac{1}{3}. iterate n=10,50,100 times the assignment: x=4x-1. what can be observed?

title: important numbers
text:
Exercise 1  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-6,k_{+}=3,t=4\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{128}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{8}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& \frac{15}{2}\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{9}{8}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{15}{16}\end{array}}




Exercise 2  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-5,k_{+}=7,t=6\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{64}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{32}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& 126\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{33}{32}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{63}{64}\end{array}}




Exercise 3  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-7,k_{+}=5,t=7\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{256}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{64}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& \frac{127}{4}\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{65}{64}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{127}{128}\end{array}}




Exercise 4  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-8,k_{+}=8,t=4\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{512}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{8}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& 240\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{9}{8}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{15}{16}\end{array}}




Exercise 5  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-8,k_{+}=4,t=6\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{512}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{32}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& \frac{63}{4}\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{33}{32}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{63}{64}\end{array}}




Exercise 6  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-8,k_{+}=5,t=5\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{512}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{16}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& 31\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{17}{16}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{31}{32}\end{array}}




Exercise 7  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-3,k_{+}=3,t=6\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{16}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{32}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& \frac{63}{8}\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{33}{32}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{63}{64}\end{array}}




Exercise 8  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-7,k_{+}=5,t=7\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{256}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{64}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& \frac{127}{4}\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{65}{64}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{127}{128}\end{array}}




Exercise 9  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-8,k_{+}=8,t=3\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{512}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{4}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& 224\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{5}{4}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{7}{8}\end{array}}




Exercise 10  

given the system \{a=2,k_{-}=-3,k_{+}=8,t=7\} what is \varepsilon_0,\varepsilon_1,M_{\infty},1_{+},1_{-} in it?



solution

{\begin{array}{cc}\varepsilon_0& \frac{1}{16}\\ & \\ \varepsilon_1& \frac{1}{64}\\ & \\ M_{\infty}& 254\\ & \\ 1_{+}& \frac{65}{64}\\ & \\ 1_{-}& \frac{127}{128}\end{array}}

title: leastsquares/concepts_draft
text:
Collect the data.
Select the most appropriate model.

Use the model (predicting future values of the process)

title: leastsquares/concepts_draft
text:
fitting a line

\begin{gather*}
\varphi_{1}(t)=1\ \ \varphi_{2}(t)=t\\
\\
A=\left[
\begin{array}{cc}
1 & t_1\\
1 & t_2\\
\vdots & \vdots\\
1 & t_{m-1}\\
1 & t_{m}\\
\end{array}
\right]\\
\\
\text{the GN:}\ \
\left[
\begin{array}{cc}
m & \sum t\\
\sum t & \sum t^2\\
\end{array}
\right]=
\left[
\begin{array}{c}
\sum f\\
\sum tf\\
\end{array}
\right]
\end{gather*}

title: leastsquares/concepts_draft
text:
fitting a parabola

\begin{gather*}
\varphi_{1}(t)=1\ \ \varphi_{2}(t)=t \ \ \varphi_{3}(t)=t^2\\
\\
A=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & t_1 & t_1^2\\
1 & t_2 & t_1^2\\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots\\
1 & t_{m-1} & t_{m-1}^2\\
1 & t_{m} & t_{m}^2\\
\end{array}
\right]\\
\\
\text{the GN:}\ \
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc}
m & \sum t & \sum t^2\\
\sum t & \sum t^2 & \sum t^3\\
\sum t^2 & \sum t^3 & \sum t^4\\
\end{array}
\right]=
\left[
\begin{array}{c}
\sum f\\
\sum tf\\
\sum t^2 f\\
\end{array}
\right]
\end{gather*}

title: leastsquares/concepts_draft
text:
matlab

for polynomial fitting the polyfit, polyval functions are used.
the polynomials in matlab represented by a vector, the main coefficient is the first, the constant is the last element - the are represented by decreasing degree coefficients
the form is classical/traditional (as there are other forms too) 
\begin{align*}
\text{vector:}& \ \ [p_{n-1},\ldots,p_0]\\
\text{polynomial:}& \ \ p_{n-1}x^{n-1}+p_{n-2}x^{n-2}+\ldots+p_{1}x+p_0\\
\end{align*}

text:
matlab is a commercial (non-free) software
for the students/staff of our university local installation is or online usage is possible after registration

title: linsys/linalg_def
text:
lpm

the k-th leading principal minor of a n\times n matrix A is det(B) where B is created by removing the last n-k rows and columns from A



dd

diagonally dominant
a matrix A is called dd if for each |a_{ii}| \ge \sum_{j\neq q}|a_{ij}| for each i
strictly dd: change \ge to >
sometimes they define it for column absulte sums

text:

whose main diagonal (downward) is [3,4,\ldots,101,102] and every other elements are zero
whose anti-diagonal (downward) is [3,4,\ldots,101,102] and every other elements are zero

\begin{array}{ccc}

1 & 0 & 1\\
1 & 1 & 1\\
\end{array}

of size 10\times 10 whose border elements are 1’s and every other elements are zero

\end{array}
\right]

\begin{array}{cccc}

0 & 0 & 1 & 1\\
1 & 1 & 0 & 0\\
1 & 1 & 0 & 0\\
\end{array}
\right]

that is the 20\times 20 version of the previous one: its 10\times 10 corners are 0’s and 1’s 
of size 20\times 20 whose imshow is: 

\left[
\begin{array}{cccc}

2 & 1 & 2 & 3\\
3 & 2 & 1 & 2\\
4 & 3 & 2 & 1\\
\end{array}
\right]

of size 10\times 10 whose imshow is: 

use the numbers [1,2,\ldots,9,10]/11 as colors starting from the main diagonal)




write a function …

that returns the 1-norm of a given matrix!

write a program/script …

that plots the unit circle of \Vert . \Vert_{1}
that plots the unit circle of \Vert . \Vert_{2}
that plots the unit circle of \Vert . \Vert_{\infty}

title: norm/notes_norm
norm/notes_norm
text:
\infty-norm
\begin{gather*}
\vert(Ax)_{i}\vert=
\vert\sum_{j} a_{ij} x_{j}\vert\le
\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \vert x_{j}\vert\le
\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \Vert x\Vert_{\infty}\le
\Vert A\Vert_{\infty} \vert \Vert x\Vert_{\infty}
\end{gather*} if the maximum is attained in the i-th row then for the sign vector of that row equality holds.

title: norm/notes_norm
text:
1-norm
\begin{gather*}
\sum_{i}\vert(Ax)_{i}\vert\le
\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \vert a_{ij}\vert \vert x_{j}\vert=
\sum_{j} \vert x_{j}\vert \sum_{i} \vert a_{ij}\vert \le
\sum_{j} \vert x_{j}\vert \Vert A\Vert_{1} \le
\Vert x\Vert_{1} \Vert A\Vert_{1}
\end{gather*} if the maximum is attained in the j-th column then for the vector e_{j} the two sides are equal.

title: norm/notes_norm
text:
special vectors
the computations above provide a way to find a vector x for which \Vert Ax\Vert=\Vert A\Vert\Vert x\Vert in the cases of 1 and \infty norms.
